questions.prompt: Which of the following are INDIRECT costs of financial distress (vs direct)?
choices: Asset fire sales below fair value
Manager time absorbed by creditor negotiations
questions.explanation: Direct = legal/accounting/court fees. Indirect = lost customers, fire sales, distracted management, employee defections, debt overhang. Indirect costs are typically much larger.

questions.prompt: Trade-off theory of capital structure says the optimal D/V is where:
choices: Marginal tax-shield benefit equals marginal expected distress cost
Cost of debt equals cost of equity
questions.explanation: At the optimum, the marginal benefit of adding a dollar of debt (tax shield) equals the marginal cost (extra distress risk). That's the trade-off.

questions.prompt: Why do knowledge-intensive firms (tech, pharma) typically carry less debt than utilities?
choices: Distress costs for intangible-heavy firms are much higher — losing key talent and customers permanently destroys IP value that has no replacement cost
Their cost of debt is higher
questions.explanation: Utilities can be sold at near-book value in distress; tech firms whose key engineers and customers walk away lose IP value that can't be reconstructed. Higher distress costs → lower optimal leverage.

questions.prompt: Debt overhang: a firm has $100M existing debt, asset value $80M, and could fund a new project requiring $20M new equity that produces $30M PV. Why might equity holders refuse to fund it?
choices: Because the project has negative NPV
Because the $30M of new value goes to debt holders first; equity holders pay $20M to get only ~$10M after debt is repaid
Because the project's risk is too high
questions.explanation: Debt overhang: positive-NPV project gets rejected because debt holders capture the value of the new investment. A leverage friction unique to distressed firms.

furtherReading.title: Trade-Off Theory of Capital Structure: Definition + Factors
furtherReading.why: Balances the tax-shield benefit against bankruptcy and debt-overhang costs in plain language, with the optimal-leverage diagram.
